topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The turpentine fumes were thick in the studio, clinging to the back of my throat like damp velvet. Another canvas stared back, mocking me with its emptiness. I slumped onto the stool, the cheap wood groaning under my sudden weight. My hand, usually nimble with the brush, felt heavy, unresponsive. I was staring at the intricate line work on the piece—a swirling depiction of a storm at sea—when I noticed it. A tiny, almost hidden mark near the anchor. It wasn't part of my composition. It was a tattoo. A deliberate, almost arrogant addition to my carefully constructed chaos. A slow, cold dread began to pool in my stomach, making my breath catch. Who had dared to claim a piece of my vision?

[story 2]

I kept rubbing my eyes, the light from the single bare bulb assaulting my tired vision. Every muscle in my neck felt like it had been knotted with old rope. The silence of the apartment was oppressive, amplifying the drumming anxiety in my chest. I was cleaning brushes, a mindless, repetitive motion, when I saw it on the edge of the preliminary sketch I’d left drying: a stylized raven, perfectly rendered, inked onto the paper like a permanent signature. It wasn't my style. It was too precise, too confident. A strange, prickly sensation crawled up my arms. I picked up the sketch, my fingers trembling slightly, and felt a deep, unsettling sense of violation.

[story 3]

The paint was sludge now, smeared across my palette knife, reflecting back a distorted image of my own slumped posture. I hadn't moved in hours, just existing in this haze of exhaustion. It was while scraping off a stubborn layer of Prussian blue that I saw the detail. Embedded within the shadow of the tempest I was trying to capture, a small, stark symbol—a geometric knot—was undeniably inked onto the canvas. It was undeniable. Someone had visited. The realization hit me with a dull thud, leaving a hollow ache behind my ribs. I slowly lowered the knife, the sound unnaturally loud in the quiet room, feeling every nerve ending tighten into a single, taut wire.
